ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific entry for Singaporean-style condensed milk tea with reduced sugar. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers tea with milk as the closest defensible category; no specific code for 'siu dai' (less sweet) variant. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Singaporean-style reduced-sugar condensed milk tea.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Teh Siu Dai is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to one cup or glass as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre or kopitiam.

nutrient_source.title: Teh Siu Dai — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Teh Siu Dai (cr_id D09020074). Confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Saturated fat estimated from HPB milk tea composition data for the condensed milk component. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and all micronutrient fields are not individually reported by HPB for this item and are set to -1 as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — milk tea / sweetened tea with milk analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Teh Siu Dai in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to sweetened milk tea with condensed milk entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and supporting peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). A GI of 55 falls within the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification system. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (17.5 g per 250 g serving) / 100 = 9.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar from condensed milk (6.71 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; independently associated with elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Teh Siu Dai and opt for Teh Kosong (unsweetened black tea) to eliminate the sugar-driven blood pressure risk, as even reduced-sugar variants remain above the safe beverage sugar threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.71 g sugar per 100 g; liquid sugar raises postprandial blood glucose rapidly and qualifies as an SSB under ADA guidelines regardless of the 'less sweet' designation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Teh Siu Dai entirely and substitute with Teh Kosong (no sugar, no condensed milk) or request evaporated milk without added sugar to eliminate the glycaemic spike risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Condensed milk contributes saturated fat (~1.0 g/100 g) and added sugar (6.71 g/100 g); regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk (AHA 2021).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid Teh Siu Dai due to its added sugar and saturated fat content, and prefer Teh Kosong or unsweetened tea with low-fat evaporated milk as a lower-risk alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.71 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; saturated fat from condensed milk (~1.0 g/100 g) further contributes to systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Teh Siu Dai and choose Teh Kosong with evaporated milk to benefit from black tea polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh Siu Dai is a popular Singaporean kopitiam (coffee shop) beverage consisting of strong brewed black tea mixed with condensed milk and a reduced quantity of sugar — 'siu dai' meaning 'less sweet' in Hokkien/Cantonese. It is a variant of the classic Teh (Singapore milk tea), prepared by brewing robust Ceylon or Robusta-blend black tea, then combining it with sweetened condensed milk and a smaller-than-standard measure of sugar. The result is a warm, creamy, mildly sweet tea with a rich malty flavour and smooth texture. Per 100 g, Teh Siu Dai provides approximately 46 kcal, 0.75 g protein, 1.64 g fat (predominantly saturated from condensed milk), and 7.0 g carbohydrate of which 6.71 g is sugar. Sodium is low at 23 mg/100 g. Despite the 'less sweet' label, the beverage still qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA and AHA definitions, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low GI by University of Sydney classification) by analogy to sweetened condensed milk tea. Clinically, the primary concern is the added sugar load from condensed milk, which drives postprandial glucose elevation and is independently associated with elevated blood pressure. Teh Kosong (no sugar, no milk) is the recommended substitute for health-conscious consumers. Teh Siu Dai is a staple of Singapore breakfast and snack culture, commonly ordered at hawker centres and kopitiams alongside kaya toast or roti.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink Teh Siu Dai?
How many calories in Teh Siu Dai?
What is the difference between Teh and Teh Siu Dai?
Is Teh Siu Dai suitable for hypertension?
How much sugar is in Teh Siu Dai?
What is less sweet milk tea in Singapore?
Teh Siu Dai vs Teh Kosong nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020074) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the canonical input record. HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened condensed milk tea, clearly documented.
notes: Saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g) is estimated from HPB milk tea composition data for the condensed milk component; the HPB Foodo record for this item does not separately report saturated fat. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for Teh Siu Dai are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as of 2026: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g. Sources consulted and confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables — neither database reports these micronutrient and fatty acid sub-fractions for this specific Singaporean tea beverage preparation. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened condensed milk tea entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care); GI 55 is classified as low GI (≤55) per University of Sydney classification. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to accurately reflect the University of Sydney GI boundary (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input record are retained as contextually accurate (sodium 23 mg/100 g; energy 46 kcal/100 g) but do not override the SSB health concern flags for diabetes and hypertension.
